[{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import re

# Get the file paths from the previous results
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']

# Load the full data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Extract publication years from details field
pub_years = []
book_ids = []

for book in books_data[:100]:  # Start with first 100 to test extraction
    details = book.get('details', '')
    
    # Look for year patterns in details
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b', details)
    
    if year_matches:
        year = int(year_matches[0])
        pub_years.append(year)
        book_ids.append(book['book_id'])

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'num_books_total': len(books_data),
    'num_reviews_total': len(reviews_data),
    'extracted_years': pub_years[:20],
    'sample_books': books_data[:3]
}))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re

# Get file paths from previous results
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']

# Load full data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year for each book
book_years = {}
invalid_books = 0

for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    book_id = book['book_id']
    
    # Find year patterns
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b', details)
    
    if year_matches:
        year = int(year_matches[0])
        # Check if it's a reasonable range
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            book_years[book_id] = year
        else:
            invalid_books += 1
    else:
        invalid_books += 1

# Calculate decade for each book
book_decades = {}
for book_id, year in book_years.items():
    decade = (year // 10) * 10
    book_decades[book_id] = decade

# Map purchase_id to book_id
# They match by pattern: bookid_X -> purchaseid_X
purchase_to_book = {}
for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    # Extract the number part
    num_match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', purchase_id)
    if num_match:
        num = num_match.group(1)
        book_id = f'bookid_{num}'
        purchase_to_book[purchase_id] = book_id

# Join reviews with book decades and calculate ratings by decade
decade_ratings = {}
decade_books = {}  # Track distinct books per decade
book_ratings = {}  # Track all ratings per book

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    if purchase_id in purchase_to_book:
        book_id = purchase_to_book[purchase_id]
        
        if book_id in book_decades:
            decade = book_decades[book_id]
            
            # Initialize if needed
            if decade not in decade_ratings:
                decade_ratings[decade] = []
                decade_books[decade] = set()
            
            decade_ratings[decade].append(rating)
            decade_books[decade].add(book_id)

# Calculate stats per decade
decade_stats = []
for decade in decade_ratings:
    num_books = len(decade_books[decade])
    
    if num_books >= 10:  # Only consider decades with at least 10 distinct books
        avg_rating = sum(decade_ratings[decade]) / len(decade_ratings[decade])
        total_reviews = len(decade_ratings[decade])
        
        decade_stats.append({
            'decade': f"{decade}s",
            'decade_num': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'total_reviews': total_reviews
        })

# Sort by average rating
decade_stats.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

# Get the decade with highest average rating
result = None
if decade_stats:
    result = decade_stats[0]

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'top_decade': result,
    'all_decades': decade_stats[:10],
    'total_books_with_year': len(book_years),
    'total_reviews_linked': sum(len(ratings) for ratings in decade_ratings.values())
}))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re

# Get file paths
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']

# Load full data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Extract years for all books
book_years = {}
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    book_id = book['book_id']
    
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b', details)
    if year_matches:
        year = int(year_matches[0])
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            book_years[book_id] = year

# Check mapping patterns
purchase_ids = [review['purchase_id'] for review in reviews_data[:50]]
book_ids = [book['book_id'] for book in books_data[:20]]

# Try different mapping approaches
def map_purchase_to_book(purchase_id):
    # Extract number if exists
    num_match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', purchase_id)
    if num_match:
        num = num_match.group(1)
        # Try different patterns
        candidate1 = f'bookid_{num}'
        candidate2 = f'bookid_{int(num)}'  # Just to be safe
        return candidate1
    return None

# Test mapping
sample_mappings = []
for purchase_id in purchase_ids[:10]:
    mapped = map_purchase_to_book(purchase_id)
    sample_mappings.append({
        'purchase_id': purchase_id,
        'mapped_book_id': mapped,
        'book_exists': mapped in book_years if mapped else False
    })

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'book_ids_sample': book_ids[:10],
    'purchase_ids_sample': purchase_ids[:10],
    'mappings': sample_mappings,
    'books_with_year': len(book_years)
}, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

# Get file paths

reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']

# Load full data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Extract years for all books
book_years = {}
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    book_id = book['book_id']
    
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b', details)
    if year_matches:
        year = int(year_matches[0])
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            book_years[book_id] = year

# Check mapping patterns
purchase_ids = [review['purchase_id'] for review in reviews_data[:50]]
book_ids = [book['book_id'] for book in books_data[:20]]

# Try different mapping approaches
def map_purchase_to_book(purchase_id):
    # Extract number if exists
    num_match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', purchase_id)
    if num_match:
        num = num_match.group(1)
        # Try different patterns
        candidate1 = f'bookid_{num}'
        candidate2 = f'bookid_{int(num)}'  # Just to be safe
        return candidate1
    return None

# Test mapping
sample_mappings = []
for purchase_id in purchase_ids[:10]:
    mapped = map_purchase_to_book(purchase_id)
    sample_mappings.append({

        'mapped_book_id': mapped,
        'book_exists': mapped in book_years if mapped else False
    })

    'purchase_ids_sample': purchase_ids[:10],
    'mappings': sample_mappings,

}, indent=2))

args.code:
import json
import re

# Get file paths
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']

# Load full data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Extract book numbers
book_numbers = []
for book in books_data:
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', book['book_id'])
    if match:
        book_numbers.append(int(match.group(1)))

# Extract purchase numbers  
purchase_numbers = []
for review in reviews_data:
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', review['purchase_id'])
    if match:
        purchase_numbers.append(int(match.group(1)))

# Check overlap
book_set = set(book_numbers)
purchase_set = set(purchase_numbers)
overlap = book_set.intersection(purchase_set)

# Check mapping with numbers in range
valid_reviews = []
for review in reviews_data:
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', review['purchase_id'])
    if match:
        num = int(match.group(1))
        if num <= len(books_data):  # Only include if book likely exists
            valid_reviews.append(review)

# Re-extract years
book_years = {}
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    book_id = book['book_id']
    
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b', details)
    if year_matches:
        year = int(year_matches[0])
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            book_years[book_id] = year

# Calculate decades
decade_stats = {}
for review in valid_reviews:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', purchase_id)
    if match:
        num = int(match.group(1))
        book_id = f'bookid_{num}'
        
        if book_id in book_years:
            year = book_years[book_id]
            decade = (year // 10) * 10
            
            if decade not in decade_stats:
                decade_stats[decade] = {'ratings': [], 'books': set()}
            
            decade_stats[decade]['ratings'].append(rating)
            decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_id)

# Filter and sort results
results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(stats['ratings']) / len(stats['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': f"{decade}s",
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'total_reviews': len(stats['ratings'])
        })

results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'total_books': len(books_data),
    'books_with_year': len(book_years),
    'valid_reviews_in_range': len(valid_reviews),
    'overlap_numbers': len(overlap),
    'decade_stats': results[:10],
    'top_decade': results[0] if results else None
}))

val_args.code:
import json
import re

# Get file paths
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']

# Load full data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Extract book numbers
book_numbers = []
for book in books_data:
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', book['book_id'])
    if match:
        book_numbers.append(int(match.group(1)))

# Extract purchase numbers  
purchase_numbers = []
for review in reviews_data:
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', review['purchase_id'])
    if match:
        purchase_numbers.append(int(match.group(1)))

# Check overlap
book_set = set(book_numbers)
purchase_set = set(purchase_numbers)
overlap = book_set.intersection(purchase_set)

# Check mapping with numbers in range
valid_reviews = []
for review in reviews_data:
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', review['purchase_id'])
    if match:
        num = int(match.group(1))
        if num <= len(books_data):  # Only include if book likely exists
            valid_reviews.append(review)

# Re-extract years
book_years = {}
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    book_id = book['book_id']
    
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b', details)
    if year_matches:
        year = int(year_matches[0])
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            book_years[book_id] = year

# Calculate decades
decade_stats = {}
for review in valid_reviews:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', purchase_id)
    if match:
        num = int(match.group(1))
        book_id = f'bookid_{num}'
        
        if book_id in book_years:
            year = book_years[book_id]
            decade = (year // 10) * 10
            
            if decade not in decade_stats:
                decade_stats[decade] = {'ratings': [], 'books': set()}
            
            decade_stats[decade]['ratings'].append(rating)
            decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_id)

# Filter and sort results
results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(stats['ratings']) / len(stats['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': f"{decade}s",
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'total_reviews': len(stats['ratings'])
        })

results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'total_books': len(books_data),
    'books_with_year': len(book_years),
    'valid_reviews_in_range': len(valid_reviews),
    'overlap_numbers': len(overlap),
    'decade_stats': results[:10],
    'top_decade': results[0] if results else None
}))

var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re

# Get file paths
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']

# Load full data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Extract publication years from all books
book_years = {}
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    book_id = book['book_id']
    
    if not details:
        continue
    
    year_matches = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b', details)
    if year_matches:
        year = int(year_matches[0])
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            book_years[book_id] = year

# Process all reviews and join with books
decade_stats = {}
review_count = 0
mapped_reviews = 0

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    review_count += 1
    
    # Map purchase_id to book_id by extracting the number
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)$', purchase_id)
    if match:
        num = int(match.group(1))
        # Check if this book_id exists and has year data
        book_id = f'bookid_{num}'
        
        if book_id in book_years:
            mapped_reviews += 1
            year = book_years[book_id]
            decade = (year // 10) * 10
            
            if decade not in decade_stats:
                decade_stats[decade] = {'ratings': [], 'books': set()}
            
            decade_stats[decade]['ratings'].append(rating)
            decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book_id)

# Filter for decades with at least 10 distinct books and calculate stats
decade_results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    num_books = len(stats['books'])
    if num_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(stats['ratings']) / len(stats['ratings'])
        decade_results.append({
            'decade': f"{decade}s",
            'decade_num': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'total_reviews': len(stats['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating
decade_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

# Prepare final result
top_decade = decade_results[0] if decade_results else None

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'top_decade': top_decade,
    'all_eligible_decades': decade_results,
    'summary_stats': {
        'total_books_in_dataset': len(books_data),
        'books_with_extracted_year': len(book_years),
        'total_reviews': review_count,
        'reviews_successfully_mapped': mapped_reviews,
        'decades_with_10_plus_books': len(decade_results)
    }
}))

args.code:
import json
import re

# Get books file
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Load books data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Debug year extraction carefully
book_years = {}
detailed_debug = []

for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    book_id = book['book_id']
    
    if not details:
        detailed_debug.append({'book_id': book_id, 'status': 'no_details'})
        continue
    
    # Find all 4-digit numbers that look like years
    all_numbers = re.findall(r'\d{4}', details)
    
    # Filter for years between 1900-2023
    valid_years = []
    for num in all_numbers:
        year = int(num)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            valid_years.append(year)
    
    if valid_years:
        # Take the first valid year as publication year
        pub_year = valid_years[0]
        book_years[book_id] = pub_year
        detailed_debug.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'status': 'success',
            'year': pub_year,
            'all_years_found': valid_years
        })
    else:
        detailed_debug.append({
            'book_id': book_id,
            'status': 'no_valid_year',
            'details_preview': details[:150] if details else 'empty'
        })

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'total_books': len(books_data),
    'successful_extraction': len(book_years),
    'sample_success': list(book_years.items())[:10],
    'sample_failures': [d for d in detailed_debug if d['status'] != 'success'][:5]
}, indent=2))

args.code:
import json
import re

# Get file paths
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']

# Load full data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Extract publication year for each book
book_years = {}
for book in books_data:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    book_id = book['book_id']
    
    if details:
        # Find all 4-digit numbers
        years = re.findall(r'\d{4}', details)
        # Take the first valid year between 1900-2023
        for year_str in years:
            year = int(year_str)
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                book_years[book_id] = year
                break

# Create mapping from purchase_id to book_id using the numeric suffix
# purchaseid_X -> bookid_X
purchase_to_book_map = {}
for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)$', purchase_id)
    if match:
        num = match.group(1)
        book_id = f'bookid_{num}'
        purchase_to_book_map[purchase_id] = book_id

# Aggregate ratings by decade
decade_ratings = {}  # decade -> list of ratings
decade_books = {}    # decade -> set of book_ids

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    if purchase_id in purchase_to_book_map:
        book_id = purchase_to_book_map[purchase_id]
        
        if book_id in book_years:
            year = book_years[book_id]
            decade = (year // 10) * 10
            
            if decade not in decade_ratings:
                decade_ratings[decade] = []
                decade_books[decade] = set()
            
            decade_ratings[decade].append(rating)
            decade_books[decade].add(book_id)

# Calculate statistics for decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = []
for decade in decade_ratings:
    num_books = len(decade_books[decade])
    
    if num_books >= 10:
        total_reviews = len(decade_ratings[decade])
        avg_rating = sum(decade_ratings[decade]) / total_reviews
        
        decade_stats.append({
            'decade': f"{decade}s",
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'num_books': num_books,
            'total_reviews': total_reviews
        })

# Sort by average rating (highest first)
decade_stats.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

# Get the decade with highest average rating
top_decade = decade_stats[0] if decade_stats else None

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'top_decade': top_decade,
    'all_eligible_decades': decade_stats,
    'summary': {
        'total_books_with_year': len(book_years),
        'total_reviews_processed': len(reviews_data),
        'mapped_reviews': sum(len(ratings) for ratings in decade_ratings.values()),
        'decades_eligible': len(decade_stats)
    }
}))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!
